content:
First, observe that if \(n\) has \(k\) digits then its digit‐product satisfies
\[
P(n)\;\le\;9^k.
\]
Meanwhile
\[
n^2-15n-27\;=\;n(n-15)-27
\]
is negative for \(1\le n\le16\), zero only at non‐integer roots, and grows quadratically for \(n\ge17\).  Hence:

1.  For \(1\le n\le16\), the right‐hand side is \(<0\) while the product of digits is \(\ge0\), so no solutions there.

2.  For \(n\ge17\), we need
   \[
   n^2-15n-27\;=\;P(n)\;\le\;9^k.
   \]
   If \(n\) is two‐digit (\(10\le n\le99\)), then \(P(n)\le81\), so
   \[
   n^2-15n-27\le81
   \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
   n\le20.3\ldots
   \]
   Thus we only need to check \(n=17,18,19,20\).  A quick check shows only
   \(n=17\) works:
   \[
     1\cdot7=7,
     \quad 17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7.
   \]

3.  For \(n\ge21\), \(n^2-15n-27>81\ge P(n)\), so no further solutions.

Hence the only solution is \(n=17\), and the sum of all such \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{17}.
\]

1.  For \(1\le n\le16\), the right‐hand side is \(<0\) while the product of digits is \(\ge0\), so no solutions there.

   \[
   n^2-15n-27\;=\;P(n)\;\le\;9^k.
   \]

   \[
   n^2-15n-27\le81
   \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
